text: Hi, I am Dr. Amar, metabolic and bariatric surgeon from Hyderabad. This is a video of single anastomosis sleeve ileal bypass. It is also known as sleeve gastrectomy with loop bipartition. It is a modification of SAD-ES. Difference being, duodenum is not transacted in this procedure and terminal ileum is directly anastomosed to pyloric antrum. in a loop fashion. Entry is by using right upper parodactyl, twelve mm optical port.

The surgeon adjusts the optical port parallel to the abdominal wall upon visualizing the peritoneum, preparing to insert it further. Three ports are utilized: a 12 mm optical port in the right upper quadrant, a 10 mm umbilical port, and a 5 mm port in the left upper quadrant.

The surgeon disconnects the greater omentum from the greater curvature of the stomach using an energy source, starting opposite and just above the level of the incisura angularis. The lesser sac is entered, and the resection continues upwards towards the gastroesophageal junction.

The surgeon uses a bowel grasper to retract the stomach and liver, ensuring adequate visualization without the need for an additional port. The greater omentum is retracted effectively with proper technique.

The surgeon divides all short gastric vessels, taking care with the upper short gastric vessels. The pancreaticogastric peritoneal reflection and attachments are carefully divided to fully mobilize the gastric fundus.

The surgeon continues dissection up to the gastroesophageal junction, ensuring the gastroesophageal pad of fat remains intact to preserve blood supply to the upper sleeve. During the division between the stomach and pancreas, the left gastric vessels and splenic artery are visible, requiring careful navigation.

The surgeon continues dissection until the left crus of the diaphragm is identified, proceeding towards the pylorus. Care is taken to stay away from the greater curvature to avoid damaging the sleeve. The greater omentum is disconnected from the greater curvature of the stomach up to the pylorus to minimize bleeding during anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the relief of pancreaticogastric attachments to fully mobilize the pyloric antrum. The devascularization of the greater curvature is visibly complete. Gastric sleeve formation begins six centimeters proximal to the pylorus, with the pyloric antrum measuring six centimeters in width to ensure proper horizontal gastro-ileal anastomosis. The first stapler, a sixty green, is fired, and a gastric calibration tube is passed following this step.

The surgeon uses Purple-sixty tri staplers to staple the upper part of the stomach, retracting the liver with the right hand instrument. The stapling is done in increments of two millimeters with pauses to ensure effective tissue compression and minimal bleeding. The fundus is completely removed, staying at least one centimeter away from the gastroesophageal junction. The divided stomach is placed in the right upper quadrant, anterior to the liver, and an anchoring stitch is taken near the pylorus using a 3-0 PDS suture.

The surgeon divides the omentum into two halves at the site of the proposed anastomosis to reduce tension. The omentum is disconnected from the transverse colon over a few inches to allow the anastomotic loop to lie comfortably. The ileocecal junction is identified, and the terminal ileum is measured backwards over a length of 250 to 300 centimeters to reach the point of anastomosis. A storage bowl grasper is used for bubble measurement, with markings indicating five centimeters increments for precise measurement.

The surgeon uses a bubble grasper with a ratchet through the right upper quadrant port to measure and push the measured bubble into the right lower quadrant. A 300-centimeter segment of the terminal ileum is measured by counting sixty times. The surgeon takes a bite on the ileal loop using 3-0 PDS and applies a knot to approximate the pylorus and ileal loop, preparing for a two-layer anastomosis: an outer seromuscular layer and an inner full-thickness layer.

The surgeon begins suturing the posterior seromuscular layer at the pylorus, moving towards the staple line to approximate the anteroinferior aspect of the pyloric antrum to the ileal loop near the mesenteric border. The suture is tightened periodically for effective approximation, and a knot is applied at the left end to secure the layer.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel or ligature Maryland to create an opening in the gastric sleeve and ileal loop, guided by a calibration tube. A gastrotomy is performed over a length of 3 to 4.5 centimeters, avoiding monopolar cautery to minimize bleeding risk. The ileum is opened on the anti-mesenteric border for a corresponding length. A 3-0 PDS suture is used to create a posterior full-thickness layer from left to right, ensuring inclusion of the gastric mucosa and serosa of both the stomach and ileum in the suture bites.
The surgeon tightens the suture intermittently during the anastomosis. A 45 mm tri-stapler is used to perform the anastomosis, which can be oriented either horizontally or vertically.

The surgeon applies a knot to secure the posterior layer on the right side and continues suturing the anterior full thickness layer from right to left, ensuring inclusion of gastric mucosa in the bites while avoiding vaginal mucosa. Bites are taken beyond the blanched area on the serosa, carefully avoiding blood vessels, and the suture is tightened intermittently for a secure anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the inner layer of the anastomosis by applying a knot at the left end. The posterior seromuscular layer is continued as the anterior seromuscular layer, starting from the left side and moving towards the right side, effectively burying the anterior inner layer. A knot is applied at the right end to complete the outer layer, finalizing the anastomosis. Anchoring sutures are applied on either side for additional security, while the wide gap behind the anastomosis is left open.

The surgeon uses methylene blue solution to test the potency of the anastomosis. The gastric sleeve staple line and anastomosis are covered with surgical material. The stomach is removed and placed in a sterile plastic bag, revealing the sleeve with two outlets.

Since most of the undigested food reaches the terminal ileum through anastomosis, hormonal changes are high. So weight loss will be more, weight regain risk is less, diabetes resolution is more. Since part of the food goes through duodenum also, malabsorption will be less. Surgery is easy to perform, learning curve is short and access to biliary tract is maintained. Hypoglycemia risk is less because anti-incretin effect is still maintained. Please like this video on YouTube. Thank you.
